title: Using rabbit manure in the garden
subtitle: A cold manure that goes straight onto beds without composting first.
excerpt: Rabbit droppings are one of the few animal manures that can be applied directly to growing beds without prior composting. The nitrogen content is moderate, the pellets are easy to handle, and unlike chicken or pig manure they don't burn roots. A rabbit colony produces more than enough to fertilise a kitchen garden.
sourceNotes: HDRA/Garden Organic composting guidance, gardenorganic.org.uk. Soil Association organic growing notes. Emery, C., Encyclopedia of Country Living (updated edition), for smallholding integration.

glossaryTerms.definition: Animal manure with a low nitrogen-to-carbon ratio that does not generate significant heat during decomposition and can be applied without burning plant roots. Rabbit, horse, and cow manure are cold; chicken and pig manure are hot.

content.text: . Its nitrogen content is about 2.4 percent, potassium around 0.5 percent, and phosphorous around 1.4 percent, comparable to general-purpose pelleted fertiliser in a natural slow-release form. The pellet form is easy to handle and distributes evenly when scattered by hand or raked in.

Fresh rabbit droppings can be scattered directly onto garden beds as a top dressing or raked into the surface before planting. Use a rate of approximately two to three handfuls per square metre. No delay is needed before planting, the pellets are not hot enough to damage roots even in direct contact.
The most effective application is worked lightly into the top 5 cm of soil rather than left on the surface, where it dries to powder and blows away.

Rabbit manure collected with straw or hay bedding adds nitrogen to a carbon-heavy compost heap and speeds decomposition. Layer it with vegetable scraps, grass clippings, or garden waste. A heap with rabbit manure incorporated every week or two will reach usable compost in three to four months in summer.

A handful of droppings soaked in a bucket of water for twenty-four hours makes a dilute liquid feed that can be applied around the base of growing plants. The resulting liquid should be straw-coloured; anything darker is too concentrated and should be diluted further. Use once a week during the growing season on heavy-feeding crops like brassicas and squash.

Rabbit manure is most nutritious when the rabbits have been on a mixed diet of hay, fresh greens, and pellets. Manure from rabbits on pellet-only diets is lower in organic matter and breaks down more quickly without improving soil structure. The quality of the output reflects the quality of the input.

Collect droppings when you clean the hutch or colony pen and put them straight onto the heap or into a covered bucket for use within the week. Rabbit manure stored uncovered loses nitrogen to the air quickly; a lid or tarpaulin reduces the loss.
Four meat rabbits produce around 50 kg of manure a year. That is enough to fertilise a vegetable plot of 20 to 25 square metres.
